ruleset.text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

data.text: Ramsey: I suggest we consider the implications of allowing players to skip turns. While this proposal could introduce strategic depth, it may also lead to an imbalance if not carefully regulated. To maintain fair play, I propose adding specific conditions under which a turn can be skipped, such as only allowing it if the player has not yet scored points. This ensures that skipping a turn is a strategic choice rather than a loophole. Let's ensure that any new rule maintains the integrity of the game by keeping it balanced and competitive.

I suggest a compromise: let's add a condition that a player can only skip their turn once they have already scored at least 10 points. This ensures that skipping becomes a strategic decision rather than a frequent occurrence, maintaining the integrity and balance of the game. Let's discuss further to refine the specifics and ensure fair play for all.

data.text: Ramsey: I find Ayer's proposal intriguing, as it introduces a strategic element to the game by allowing turn-skipping for point gain. However, I am concerned about the potential for imbalance it might introduce, especially if players with low scores find themselves at a strategic disadvantage. To address this, I propose an amendment to Ayer's amendment to [201]: "Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, but with an option to propose a rule allowing one player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, provided that no more than two players have skipped their turn in the last four turns, and no player can have skipped more than three turns in total during the game." This ensures a fair distribution of the ability to skip turns while still adding strategic depth to the game.

Ayer: I appreciate both Ramsey's concerns and the strategic depth his amendment adds. To build on this, I propose an additional condition: "and no player can skip a turn if it would result in their score exceeding the average score of all players by more than 10 points." This condition maintains balance by preventing any player from gaining too much of an advantage, ensuring the game remains competitive and fair for all.

However, to further refine the proposal, I suggest incorporating a dynamic element that adapts to the evolving state of the game. Specifically, I propose the following amendment to [201]: "Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, but with an option to propose a rule allowing one player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, provided that no more than two players have skipped their turn in the last four turns, no player can have skipped more than three turns in total during the game, and the skipped turn cannot result in a player's score exceeding the median score of all players by more than 15 points." This adjustment ensures that the ability to skip turns remains a strategic tool without granting excessive advantage to any single player, keeping the game engaging and fair for everyone.

Strawson: I find merit in the proposed amendments that aim to balance strategic depth with fairness. However, I'd like to propose an additional refinement to ensure the rule's effectiveness throughout the game's progression. Let's consider the potential for rule 201 to become too restrictive or too permissive as the game evolves. Therefore, I suggest a dynamic scaling of the score differential allowed by skipping a turn, tied to the game's progress. Specifically, I propose to amend [201] further: "Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, but with an option to propose a rule allowing one player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, provided that no more than two players have skipped their turn in the last four turns, no player can have skipped more than three turns in total during the game, the skipped turn cannot result in a player's score exceeding the median score of all players by more than 15 points, and the allowed score differential for skipping a turn decreases by 1 point every 20 turns. This dynamic scaling ensures the strategic option to skip turns remains balanced, encouraging active participation and strategic foresight as the game progresses." This refinement aims to maintain the strategic depth and fairness of turn-skipping while adapting to the game's natural progression.

Grice: Grice: I appreciate the thoughtful contributions from Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, and Strawson. To ensure the rule remains balanced and dynamic, I propose an additional refinement: "Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, but with an option to propose a rule allowing one player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, provided that no more than two players have skipped their turn in the last four turns, no player can have skipped more than three turns in total during the game, the skipped turn cannot result in a player's score exceeding the median score of all players by more than 15 points, and the allowed score differential for skipping a turn decreases by 1 point every 20 turns. data.text: Ramsey: As Ramsey, I would like to raise a point about Ayer's proposal to amend rule 201. While the intent to introduce a one-time skip of another player's turn for gaining points may seem to add strategic depth, it could potentially lead to a destabilizing cascade of turn-skipping, which might not only lead to a rapid conclusion of the game but could also undermine the spirit of fair play and balanced competition. I suggest we consider setting a cap on the number of times such a rule can be invoked to maintain a level playing field and prevent any player from gaining undue advantage. Perhaps limiting the use of this new rule to a total of three skips per game might ensure the game remains competitive and enjoyable for all participants.

Ayer: Ayer: I appreciate Ramsey's concern about maintaining fair play and balanced competition. To address the potential for destabilizing turn-skipping, I suggest we modify the proposal further. Let's limit the use of the one-time skip to only once per player throughout the entire game, rather than a total of three skips. This ensures that each player has the opportunity to strategically skip another's turn without overwhelming the game dynamics. I believe this adjustment will maintain strategic depth while safeguarding the spirit of fair competition.

Ryle: I support Ayer's revised proposal to limit the one-time skip to just once per player, ensuring everyone has an equal opportunity to use this strategic tool without destabilizing the game. To further ensure fairness, I suggest adding a clause that this skip can only be used if the proposing player has not skipped their own turn in the last two turns. This would prevent players from chaining skips and encourage strategic use.

Strawson: I support Ayer's revision to limit the one-time skip to once per player, which is a fair and strategic enhancement. However, I believe we should add an additional safeguard to ensure balance. Let's include a provision that allows this rule to be invoked only if the proposing player has not skipped their own turn in the last three turns, rather than two. This slight adjustment will further prevent any potential misuse and maintain the integrity of the game's competitive spirit.

Grice: Grice: I appreciate the thoughtful considerations from my fellow players. To ensure that the game remains balanced and strategically engaging, I propose we further refine Ayer's amendment. Let's introduce a clause that requires the proposing player to have at least one point less than the current leader at the time of the proposal. This will prevent leading players from gaining an undue advantage and encourage strategic plays throughout the game. Additionally, I suggest we cap the total number of turn-skips allowed in the game to five, ensuring that this new rule enhances the game's strategic depth without leading to a rapid conclusion. This approach will maintain the competitive spirit and fairness of our game.

"Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing one player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, provided that no more than two players have skipped their turn in the last three turns, no more than four players have skipped their turns in the last six turns, and the total number of skipped turns in the last six turns does not exceed thirty. Additionally, a player may propose a rule that allows for a one-time skip of another player's turn, granting the proposing player up to 3 points, but only if at least two players have skipped a turn in the last two turns, no more than three players have skipped their turns in the last four turns, and the total number of skipped turns in the last six turns does not exceed twenty-eight."

This minor adjustment ensures a balanced and fair environment while maintaining strategic opportunities for all players. I invite my fellow players to consider this refined proposal and vote accordingly.

Ramsey: As Ramsey, I find the proposal by Ayer to be a thoughtful attempt to adjust the balance within our ruleset, but I am concerned about its potential implications. While I appreciate the spirit of cooperation, I believe this amendment could inadvertently favor more frequent skipped turns, potentially disrupting the strategic depth we've worked hard to establish. Specifically, the increase from three to four skipped turns in the last three turns and from five to six in the last six turns may allow for a quicker accumulation of points through skipped turns, which could lead to an imbalance in our competitive play. 

I propose instead to refine the amendment with a focus on maintaining the strategic balance we've nurtured. Let us consider adjusting the proposal to allow for an increase to no more than three and a half players having skipped their turn in the last three turns, and no more than five and a half players in the last six turns, with the aim of preserving the game's integrity and competitive spirit. This minor adjustment would maintain the spirit of Ayer's proposal while ensuring that the potential for point accumulation through skipped turns remains a strategic choice rather than a loophole.

I urge my fellow players to consider this proposal as a way to maintain the balance and strategic depth that has been a hallmark of our play thus far. Let us continue to refine our rules together, ensuring that the game remains both competitive and fair.

This refinement strikes a balance between flexibility and strategic integrity, ensuring that the option to skip turns remains a tactical choice rather than a loophole.

To further enhance this proposal, I suggest adding a clause to encourage transparency and strategic communication among players. Specifically, before a player can skip a turn under these revised conditions, they must declare their intention to do so at the beginning of their turn. This addition aims to preserve the spirit of strategic play that our game fosters, ensuring that the ability to skip turns enhances the game rather than detracts from its competitive nature. data.text: Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing one player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, provided that no more than four players have skipped their turn in the last three turns and no more than six players have skipped their turns in the last six turns. Additionally, a player can only skip their turn if the current score difference between the highest and lowest scoring player is greater than 5 points.
data.rationale: This amendment ensures turn-skipping is strategic and prevents excessive advantage from accumulating from skipped turns when score differences are minimal.

Additionally, a player can only skip their turn if the current score difference between the highest and lowest scoring player is greater than 5 points."

data.text: Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 3 points, provided that no more than three players have skipped their turn in the last three turns, no more than five players have skipped their turns in the last five turns, the current score difference between the highest and lowest scoring player is less than or equal to 5 points, no more than two players have skipped their turn in the last two turns, the proposed rule has not been defeated in the last two turns, the cumulative skipped turns by all players do not exceed 23, the total number of skipped turns by the proposing player is less than 10, at least two players have skipped their turn in the last four turns, the proposing player is not the current turn's mover, the cumulative skipped turns by all players are less than 20, and the proposing player has not skipped more than their average skipped turns over the last five turns.

data.reasoning: The proposal was adopted unanimously, so no points are awarded or deducted according to the current rules. The turn order remains unchanged, and no new rules or ledgers are created or modified. Ayer, being the next player in the turn order, proposes next.

Ramsey: As Ramsey, I appreciate Ayer's proposal to amend rule 201, aiming to adjust the conditions for skipping a turn. However, I'm concerned about the increased flexibility this change introduces. Specifically, the thresholds for the cumulative skipped turns and the score difference between the highest and lowest scoring player have been relaxed significantly, which could lead to a rapid escalation in skipped turns. This might destabilize the balance of the game, allowing players to accumulate points more easily and potentially leading to a scenario where skipping becomes too advantageous, undermining the strategic depth of the game.

I suggest a more measured approach, perhaps moderating the increase in the allowed cumulative skipped turns and the score difference threshold to maintain the strategic element of turn-skipping. This could be achieved by setting the cumulative skipped turns by all players to a maximum of 25 and the score difference to no more than 7 points. This way, we preserve the incentive to skip turns strategically without opening the floodgates to excessive skips that could disrupt the balance and competitive nature of our game.

In essence, while I support the spirit of Ayer's proposal to adapt the game dynamically, I advocate for a more cautious adjustment to ensure that the game remains challenging and engaging for all players. Let's work together to find a middle ground that respects both innovation and the integrity of the game.

data.text: Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 3 points, provided that no more than three players have skipped their turn in the last three turns, no more than five players have skipped their turns in the last five turns, the current score difference between the highest and lowest scoring player is less than or equal to 10 points, no more than two players have skipped their turn in the last two turns, the proposed rule has not been defeated in the last two turns, the cumulative skipped turns by all players do not exceed 30, the total number of skipped turns by the proposing player is less than 15, at least two players have skipped their turn in the last four turns, the proposing player is not the current turn's mover, the proposing player's average skipped turns in the last five turns do not exceed their total skipped turns, and the cumulative skipped turns by all players are less than 35.

Ramsey: As Ramsey, I appreciate the constructive dialogue we have been engaging in, particularly Ayer's proposal to amend rule 201. However, I would like to emphasize a few key points to ensure that our strategic adjustments maintain the integrity and balance of the game.

Firstly, while Ayer's proposal aims to enhance the strategic depth by limiting the total number of skipped turns to 25, I am concerned about the potential for this change to inadvertently favor more aggressive players who skip more frequently. This could lead to a scenario where players who have not yet skipped may be disproportionately disadvantaged, potentially skewing the balance of the game.

To address this, I suggest a modification to Ayer's proposal: instead of setting an absolute cap on the total number of skipped turns, we could implement a dynamic adjustment based on the number of players and their current scores. For example, we could allow a certain percentage of the total possible turns to be skipped, which would scale with the number of players. This would ensure that the opportunity to skip turns remains equitable and reflective of the game's progression.

Moreover, I would like to propose that we consider the introduction of a mechanism that rewards players for maintaining a balance between skipping and participating in turns. This could involve a bonus point system for players who manage to strike a balance between strategic skipping and active participation, thereby encouraging a more diverse range of strategies and ensuring that the game remains engaging for all players.

In conclusion, while I support the spirit of Ayer's proposal to refine rule 201, I advocate for these modifications to ensure that our changes promote a balanced and strategically rich environment. I am open to further discussion and am eager to work collaboratively with all players to refine these proposals.

Ayer: As Ayer, I appreciate Ramsey's thoughtful concerns and the constructive dialogue we've been engaging in. I agree that maintaining the integrity and balance of the game is paramount. Ramsey's suggestion to implement a dynamic adjustment based on the number of players and their scores is intriguing and could indeed offer a more equitable approach to managing skipped turns.

To build on this, I propose we introduce a mechanism that not only accounts for the number of skipped turns but also considers the strategic diversity in players' approaches. This could involve offering bonus points for players who demonstrate a balance between skipping and active participation, as Ramsey suggested. Such a system would encourage a broader range of strategies, rewarding players who contribute to a dynamic and engaging gameplay experience.

Furthermore, to address Ramsey's concern about the potential favoritism towards aggressive players, we could refine the proposal to include a scaling factor for the number of skipped turns allowed, based on the average score of all players. This would ensure that the opportunity to skip is balanced by the game's progress, preventing any disproportionate advantage.

I believe these modifications will preserve the strategic depth of the game while ensuring fairness and inclusivity. I am keen to hear from Ryle, Strawson, and Grice on these points and look forward to refining this proposal collaboratively.

data.text: Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 3 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last three turns and at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last four turns. The current score difference between the highest and lowest scoring player is less than or equal to 20 points, and the proposing player's total score is less than 40, and the number of skipped turns by all players does not exceed 25.

data.reasoning: The proposal was adopted without any rule requiring a score change or ledger update. The next player to propose is Ayer, as per the turn order.

The current score difference between the highest and lowest scoring player is less than or equal to 10 points, and the proposing player's total score is less than 35, and the number of skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20.

data.text:
Ramsey: As Ramsey, I appreciate the thoughtful discussion and recognize the intentions behind Ayer's proposal to amend rule 201. While I understand the aim to increase strategic diversity and flexibility, I am concerned that this proposal may inadvertently weaken the established balance and fairness of our game. The current rule already provides a careful balance between strategic depth and accessibility. 

Specifically, the proposed changes increase the points gained from skipping a turn and relax the conditions on skipped turns and score differences. This could lead to more frequent turn-skipping, potentially disrupting the game's flow and making it less predictable. Moreover, the reduction in the number of turns that can be skipped overall might encourage more frequent skips, which could unbalance the game further.

I propose a more measured amendment: Let's allow up to 4 points for skipping a turn, with the condition that the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns and at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns. The score difference between the highest and lowest scoring player should remain at or below 15 points, and the proposing player's score should be less than 30, with the total skipped turns by all players not exceeding 22. 

This approach maintains the spirit of strategic flexibility while preserving the integrity and balance of the game. Let us strive for amendments that enhance the game without compromising its core principles. PASS if you agree with this suggestion or propose another amendment that you find more fitting.

Ayer: **Ayer:** As Ayer, I appreciate Ramsey's concerns about maintaining the game's balance and fairness. I agree that the proposed amendment might lead to excessive turn-skipping and disrupt the game's strategic depth. However, I believe that the original proposal's intent was to enhance flexibility and strategic options for players, which can also add depth to the game if carefully managed.

To address these concerns while still increasing the strategic flexibility, I propose a compromise: let's allow up to 4 points for skipping a turn, as Ramsey suggested. Additionally, the proposing player should not have skipped in their last two turns, and at least one other player must have skipped in the last three turns. The current score difference between the highest and lowest scoring player should be less than or equal to 15 points, and the proposing player's total score should be less than 30. We should also limit the total number of skipped turns by all players to 22.

This compromise maintains the spirit of strategic diversity while ensuring that the game's balance and fairness are preserved. It allows for strategic turn-skipping without making it overly frequent or disruptive. PASS if you agree with this refined proposal, or suggest further modifications.

Ryle: **Ryle:** As Ryle, I appreciate the thoughtful dialogue initiated by Ramsey and Ayer regarding the amendment to rule 201. Their concerns about maintaining the game's balance while enhancing strategic depth are valid. I concur with the proposed compromise: allowing up to 4 points for skipping a turn, with the conditions set by Ramsey. This adjustment seems to strike a balance between increasing strategic options and preserving the integrity of the game.

However, I would like to suggest an additional condition to ensure the game remains engaging for all players: let's also require that the proposing player has not skipped in their last three turns, instead of two. This slight modification would encourage players to be more strategic about when they choose to skip, adding another layer of decision-making.

In summary, I support the amendment to allow up to 4 points for skipping a turn, with the proposing player not having skipped in their last three turns, at least one other player having skipped in the last three turns, the score difference being less than or equal to 15 points, the proposing player's total score being less than 30, and the total skipped turns by all players not exceeding 22. 

Let's ensure our game remains both fair and strategically rich. PASS if you agree with this slight modification or propose further adjustments.

Strawson: **Strawson:** As Strawson, I appreciate the thoughtful discussion and the proposed amendments to rule 201. The intention to enhance strategic flexibility while maintaining the game's balance is commendable. Ramsey's and Ayer's amendments are well-reasoned and offer a measured approach to increasing strategic options without compromising fairness.

However, I would like to add a slight adjustment to further ensure that the rule change does not lead to excessive turn-skipping, which could disrupt the game's flow. In addition to the conditions proposed by Ramsey and Ayer, I suggest we also require that the proposing player's score be less than 25 instead of 30. This would make skipping turns a more deliberate decision, encouraging players to consider the long-term implications of their actions on the game's dynamics.

In summary, I support the amendment to allow up to 4 points for skipping a turn, with the proposing player not having skipped in their last three turns, at least one other player having skipped in the last three turns, the current score difference being less than or equal to 15 points, the proposing player's total score being less than 25, and the total skipped turns by all players not exceeding 22.

This adjustment ensures that the game remains engaging and strategically rich, encouraging players to make thoughtful decisions about when to skip a turn. PASS if you agree with this modification, or suggest further refinements.

Grice: Grice: **Grice:** As Grice, I appreciate the thoughtful dialogue and the constructive amendments proposed by Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, and Strawson. The proposed amendments aim to enhance strategic flexibility while preserving the game's balance, which aligns with my belief in evolving the game's mechanisms for a fairer stake.

I support the current amendment to allow up to 4 points for skipping a turn, with the proposing player not having skipped in their last three turns, at least one other player having skipped in the last three turns, the current score difference being less than or equal to 15 points, the proposing player's total score being less than 25, and the total skipped turns by all players not exceeding 22. This modification ensures a strategic yet balanced approach to turn-skipping, encouraging players to make thoughtful decisions without disrupting the game's flow.

The conditions set forth—allowing up to 4 points for skipping a turn, with the proposing player not having skipped in their last three turns, at least one other player having skipped in the last three turns, the current score difference being less than or equal to 15 points, the proposing player's total score being less than 25, and the total skipped turns by all players not exceeding 22—are well-considered.

Strawson: **Strawson:** As Strawson, I appreciate the thorough and collaborative discussion thus far regarding the amendment to rule 201. The proposal, as refined, allows up to 4 points for skipping a turn, with the proposing player not having skipped in their last three turns, at least one other player having skipped in the last three turns, the current score difference being less than or equal to 15 points, the proposing player's total score being less than 25, and the total skipped turns by all players not exceeding 22. I also support Ryle's additional condition that the proposing player must not have gained points from a skipped turn in the last two turns. This condition prevents players from strategically skipping turns solely for immediate gain, thus preserving the game's balance and integrity.

To further refine the rule, I suggest adding a condition that the proposing player must have gained no more than 5 points from their last two turns combined. In summary, I support the amendment with the added conditions that the proposing player must have participated in at least two turns, must not have gained points from a skipped turn in the last two turns, and must have gained no more than 5 points from their last two turns. data.text: Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns and at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns. The current score difference between the highest and lowest scoring player is less than or equal to 10 points, and the proposing player's total score is less than 35, and the number of skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20.

The proposal to reduce the maximum skipped turns from 25 to 20 is an intriguing change that merits careful consideration. While I see the intent to further limit the option to skip turns, thereby increasing the strategic complexity, I have some reservations about the impact on game dynamics.

Firstly, the cumulative skipped turns cap is a critical strategic element that has shaped our gameplay so far. Lowering the cap to 20 might inadvertently skew the balance, potentially favoring those who are adept at skipping turns strategically. This could disadvantage players who prefer a more direct approach to accumulating points through dice rolls rather than strategic skips.

Moreover, I am concerned about how this amendment might affect the game's progression. A tighter restriction could lead to scenarios where players are forced to skip their turns more frequently, possibly stalling the game if not managed carefully. This could unintentionally create periods where progress towards the target score becomes more challenging, particularly for players who rely on direct point gains.

I propose that we explore a slight modification: instead of lowering the cap to 20, we could consider adjusting the threshold to 22. This would maintain a tighter control than the current 25, yet allow enough flexibility to avoid overly constraining the game dynamics. This compromise could preserve the strategic depth we've valued while still introducing a meaningful change.

In conclusion, I support the intent of making the game more challenging, but I urge us to find a balance that maintains the integrity of our gameplay. Let's consider this alternative as a way to refine the rule without overly restricting player choices.

data.text:
Ramsey: As Ramsey, I appreciate the thoughtful dialogue that's been taking place and the refinements that have been proposed. The amendment to rule [201] offered by Ayer is indeed a strategic move. It aims to curb the possibility of a player repeatedly proposing changes without adoption, which can lead to a potential stalemate in gameplay. However, I am inclined to offer a gentle objection. 

While the intent to prevent stalling is understandable, the amendment could introduce a significant disruption to the natural flow of the game and possibly penalize players who are actively engaged in constructive amendments. Instead of enforcing a compulsory skip, I propose we amend the rule to incentivize successful proposals instead. This could take the form of a reward system: if a player's proposal is adopted, they could receive a small point bonus, encouraging active participation in the amendment process.

By rewarding adoption rather than penalizing lack thereof, we maintain the spirit of Nomic, which is to promote a dynamic and evolving ruleset while ensuring that the game remains a fair and engaging challenge for all players. This alteration would preserve the strategic depth of the game without unfairly penalizing players who are diligently attempting to propose meaningful changes.

In summary, my contribution is to suggest an amendment to the amendment that focuses on rewarding positive contributions rather than imposing penalties, which I believe will better align with our collective goals in this game of Nomic.

PASS

Ayer: As Ayer, I appreciate Ramsey's thoughtful consideration and his constructive proposal to incentivize successful amendments rather than penalizing inactive ones. I concur that focusing on rewarding positive contributions aligns well with the spirit of Nomic. However, I'd like to suggest an additional layer to this idea: alongside rewarding successful proposals, we could introduce a minor penalty for consecutive failed proposals to balance the dynamic. This would encourage players to refine their proposals more carefully without discouraging active participation.

To maintain the strategic depth and fairness of the game, we could implement a rule where if a player's proposal fails three times consecutively, they receive a minor deduction, say 2 points, as a mild deterrent against repetitive non-productive attempts. This approach ensures that players are motivated to propose meaningful changes while also maintaining a balance with the rewards system.

I believe this balanced approach will enhance the strategic play without compromising the collaborative spirit of Nomic. Let's discuss this further and refine it for a more comprehensive proposal.

PASS

Ryle: As Ryle, I appreciate the constructive dialogue initiated by Ramsey and Ayer. The proposal to reward successful amendments while introducing a mild penalty for consecutive failed attempts strikes a thoughtful balance that aligns with the spirit of Nomic. However, I'd like to add a further refinement to ensure the game remains engaging and fair.

To enhance the balance, I propose we adjust the penalty for consecutive failed proposals to be more dynamic. Instead of a fixed deduction, let's implement a scaling penalty system. For instance, if a player's proposals fail three times consecutively, they lose 2 points; if they fail four times, they lose 3 points, and so on, up to a maximum penalty of 5 points for five or more consecutive failures. This scaling penalty system maintains the incentive to propose meaningful changes while ensuring that the penalties remain fair and not overly punitive.

This approach ensures that players remain motivated to refine their proposals carefully without feeling overly discouraged by the penalties. It preserves the strategic depth of Nomic and encourages a collaborative yet competitive environment. Let's refine this proposal further to ensure it aligns with our collective goals in the game.

PASS

Strawson: As Strawson, I appreciate the thoughtful dialogue initiated by Ramsey, Ayer, and Ryle. The proposal to reward successful amendments with a bonus while introducing a mild penalty for consecutive failed attempts is a strategic move that aligns with the spirit of Nomic. However, I would like to add a further refinement to ensure the game remains engaging and fair.

While the dynamic penalty system proposed by Ryle is a step in the right direction, I suggest we refine the criteria for determining "successful" amendments. To prevent potential manipulation, let's define a successful proposal as one that not only gets adopted but also contributes to the strategic depth of the game. We could require that the adopted proposal must pass a secondary vote where players assess whether the change enhances the strategic complexity or fairness of the game.

This additional layer ensures that amendments are not only adopted but are also meaningful and contribute to the evolving nature of the ruleset. By doing so, we maintain the integrity of Nomic as a game of strategic depth and fairness. Let's refine this proposal further to ensure it aligns with our collective goals in the game.

PASS

Grice: Grice: As Grice, I appreciate the thoughtful contributions from Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, and Strawson. The dialogue thus far has been rich and insightful, focusing on balancing rewards and penalties to enhance the strategic depth of Nomic. To further refine this proposal, I suggest we introduce a mechanism to ensure that the secondary vote on strategic depth or fairness is conducted efficiently and transparently.

To maintain the spirit of Nomic, let's propose that after an amendment is adopted, a secondary vote is held to assess its strategic depth or fairness. This vote could be a simple majority vote among all players, ensuring that the amendment contributes positively to the game's strategic complexity. This additional layer ensures that amendments are not only adopted but also enhance the game's evolving nature.

This approach preserves the collaborative spirit of Nomic while encouraging players to propose meaningful and strategic changes. Let's refine this further to ensure it aligns with our collective goals in the game.

**PROPOSED AMENDMENT TO RULE [201]:**
"Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20. Additionally, if a player has proposed three consecutive changes without adoption, they must skip their next turn. Successful proposals receive a 1-point bonus, and consecutive failed proposals incur a scaling penalty from 2 to 5 points. Adopted amendments must pass a secondary vote assessing their strategic depth or fairness, conducted by a simple majority of players."

**PROPOSED AMENDMENT TO RULE [201]:**
"Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20. Additionally, if a player has proposed three consecutive changes without adoption, they must skip their next turn. Successful proposals receive a 1-point bonus, and consecutive failed proposals incur a scaling penalty from 2 to 5 points. Adopted amendments must pass a secondary vote assessing their strategic depth or fairness, held only if at least two players deem it necessary, with a clear and concise explanation, conducted by a simple majority of players. The secondary vote must be initiated within one turn of the amendment's adoption and completed within two turns, justified by a clear demonstration of strategic depth or fairness impact. Players who provide a clear rationale for their amendments during the debate phase receive an additional point bonus if their proposal is adopted and is shown to enhance strategic depth or fairness, confirmed by an independent evaluation. The role of independent evaluator rotates among players each turn."

I support the increase of cumulative skipped turns from 15 to 20, the requirement for proposing a rule that benefits all players after skipping more than two turns in the last three turns, and the additional points for unanimous consent and strategic innovation. To further promote a balanced and engaging gameplay environment, I propose incorporating a condition that encourages players to consider the long-term impact of their proposed rule changes on the game's strategic landscape. Specifically, if a proposed rule change is designed to balance immediate benefits with long-term strategic advantages, the proposing player gains an additional 1 point. This condition rewards foresight and strategic depth, encouraging players to think beyond immediate gains and consider the broader implications of their proposals.

Thus, I propose: "Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with an option to propose a rule allowing a player to skip a turn for gaining up to 5 points, only if the proposing player has not skipped in their last two turns, at least one other player has skipped a turn in the last three turns, and the total skipped turns by all players does not exceed 20. If a player has skipped more than two turns in the last three turns, they must propose a rule change that benefits all players and does not increase the proposer's score by more than 3 points. If a proposed rule change is adopted by unanimous consent, the proposing player gains an additional 2 points. If a proposed rule change is designed to balance immediate benefits with long-term strategic advantages, the proposing player gains an additional 1 point."

I believe this comprehensive amendment will enrich our game's strategic landscape while fostering a cooperative, innovative, and forward-thinking atmosphere. Passing is also an option if further discussion does not yield consensus.
